caveats: a: Occupation shares are not task shares and the BLS aggregate spans several machinery industries.
a: Oilfield equipment can have unusually engineered, safety-critical, and project-specific work, so the interval is deliberately wide.
rho: The NIST evidence concerns manufacturers generally, not this NAICS or LMM firms specifically.
rho: Large public-company digital offerings indicate sector direction but likely run ahead of smaller manufacturers.
e: No direct establishment-level dataset measures the intersection of external-customer repeat revenue, independence, correct classification, and the $1-10 million EBITDA band.
e: NOV is much larger and broader than the target population.
s5: Owner intentions are not completed transactions.
s5: The survey is not specific to manufacturing, this NAICS, or the EBITDA band.
q: This is an estimate of discounted retained gross benefit, not accounting margin.
q: NOV's global scale, proprietary installed base, and contract mix may imply greater retention than an LMM supplier can achieve.
d5: Long-horizon production paths do not translate one-for-one into five-year machinery orders.
d5: NOV is global and includes offshore and broader equipment exposures; water-well machinery has a distinct demand driver.
o: The estimate assumes persistent demand for accountable equipment manufacturers rather than any specific incumbent.
o: Product architectures, imports, consolidation, or customer integration could displace some operators without eliminating the industry function.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Recurring or repeat revenue from external customers through equipment programs, replacement units, spare parts, repair, technical support, field service, training, or manufacturer-linked monitoring.
lens.excluded_activities: Pumps, underground-mining drills, offshore floating platforms, captive internal work, pure distribution, and firms dominated by rentals or field services rather than manufacturing.
lens.included_activities: U.S. lower-middle-market manufacture of oil and gas field drilling and production machinery, derricks, and water-well drilling machinery, including related aftermarket activity attributable to the manufacturer.

a.bridge: Source population/quantity: May 2023 national employment for combined NAICS 3331, 3332, 3334, and 3339, where production occupations were 48.45% and assemblers and fabricators 19.37%. Transformation: mapped plausible portions of production, engineering, office, inspection, and logistics work into a wage-weighted exposed-task interval, discounting hands-on and accountable work. Error direction: the broad aggregate may overstate exposure if oilfield machinery is more customized and regulated, but may understate feasible CNC, robotic-welding, digital-engineering, and automated-inspection exposure.
caveats: Occupation shares are not task shares and the BLS aggregate spans several machinery industries.
Oilfield equipment can have unusually engineered, safety-critical, and project-specific work, so the interval is deliberately wide.
a.change_evidence: NIST reports present manufacturing uses in predictive maintenance, vision-based quality control, forecasting, scheduling, documentation, smart assembly, and digital twins, while identifying legacy integration, data quality, cost, skills, privacy, and cybersecurity as adoption constraints.
a.rationale: Fabrication, assembly, welding, inspection, material handling, planning, documentation, quoting, and some engineering tasks are exposed to current automation or AI, but field-specific engineering, low-volume project work, physical setup, and accountable inspection remain less automatable. Machinery-manufacturing occupational structure provides an empirical anchor rather than an exact-industry task study.

d5.bridge: Source population/quantity: EIA U.S. crude-oil and natural-gas production scenarios through 2050, plus NOV global rig-count, oil-price, backlog, and demand disclosures. Transformation: interpolated the directional production outlook to five years and widened for the greater elasticity of equipment, replacement, and aftermarket spending. Error direction: production can understate a replacement or technology upcycle and overstate equipment demand during capital discipline or productivity gains; global indicators can misstate U.S. LMM conditions either way.
caveats: Long-horizon production paths do not translate one-for-one into five-year machinery orders.
NOV is global and includes offshore and broader equipment exposures; water-well machinery has a distinct demand driver.
d5.change_evidence: EIA's 2026 outlook projects U.S. natural-gas production rising from 107 Bcf/d in 2025 to 133-151 Bcf/d by 2050 in most cases, while crude output slips from 13.6 million b/d to 12.4-12.7 million b/d. NOV separately reports 2025 worldwide rig count down 6.6% and WTI down 14.5%, illustrating near-term cyclicality.
d5.rationale: Year-five constant-price, constant-quality demand is balanced between a growing U.S. natural-gas production outlook and a roughly flat-to-declining crude outlook. Equipment demand is more cyclical than production volumes because it depends on drilling, completions, workovers, fleet replacement, customer capital budgets, and aftermarket utilization.

caveats: No direct establishment-level dataset measures the intersection of external-customer repeat revenue, independence, correct classification, and the $1-10 million EBITDA band.
NOV is much larger and broader than the target population.
e.change_evidence: NOV's Energy Equipment reporting demonstrates a sector model combining capital equipment with substantial aftermarket spare parts, repair, technical support, field service, training, and digital subscriptions, while the Census definition sharply separates pumps, mining drills, and offshore platforms.
e.rationale: Most independent manufacturers selling drilling, production, derrick, or water-well equipment and repeat parts/service to external customers fit the lens. Eligibility is reduced by one-off project shops without repeat revenue, captive operations, firms dominated by rentals or field services, pure distributors, and establishments whose actual products fall into neighboring NAICS codes.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 333132 ($5,113,852,029) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 333132 ($12,628,400,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1962 (margins.json[33]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Machinery EBITDA/Sales 19.62%); revenue/employee $422,086 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 333132); class <5: 116 firms, avg revenue/firm $858,319 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $168,402 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 84 firms, avg revenue/firm $2,368,917 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $464,781 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 93 firms, avg revenue/firm $4,468,677 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $876,755 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 132 firms, avg revenue/firm $15,233,455 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $2,988,804 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 43 firms, avg revenue/firm $63,965,395 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $12,550,011 -> out of $1-10M band; class 500+: 40 firms, avg revenue/firm $178,823,275 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $35,085,127 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 132

caveats: The estimate assumes persistent demand for accountable equipment manufacturers rather than any specific incumbent.
Product architectures, imports, consolidation, or customer integration could displace some operators without eliminating the industry function.
o.change_evidence: The Census definition is centered on tangible drilling and production machinery, while NOV describes continuing OEM obligations for aftermarket parts, repair, technical support, field service, training, condition monitoring, and compatibility across a large installed base.
o.rationale: Oilfield and water-well drilling machinery remains a physical, safety-relevant product that must be designed, fabricated, integrated, warranted, and supported by an accountable operator. AI and automation change how manufacturers execute those activities but do not normally eliminate the manufacturer; only a small tail is exposed to vertical integration, import substitution, product displacement, or business-model migration.

caveats: This is an estimate of discounted retained gross benefit, not accounting margin.
NOV's global scale, proprietary installed base, and contract mix may imply greater retention than an LMM supplier can achieve.
q.change_evidence: NOV states that service companies prefer and customers often demand OEM aftermarket support because incompatible products raise risk and cost; it also describes firm backlog and contract margin exposure to customer finances, technical requirements, manufacturing quality, supply-chain costs, and energy-market volatility.
q.rationale: Installed-base service relationships, OEM compatibility, engineering know-how, lead times, and backlog contracts can preserve a meaningful portion of gross automation benefit. Retention is constrained by sophisticated oilfield buyers, competitive rebids, negotiated cost reductions, penalties, scope changes, commodity-linked downturns, and customers' ability to dual-source or defer equipment.

rho.bridge: Source population/quantity: U.S. manufacturers discussed by NIST, supplemented by a large global oilfield-equipment OEM's analytics and condition-monitoring offerings. Transformation: converted current adoption and near-term expansion into the fraction of exposed tasks expected to be implemented within five years, discounted for LMM capital, data, and integration constraints. Error direction: stated intentions and large-OEM capability may overstate LMM realization, while vendor diffusion and labor pressure could make the base conservative.
caveats: The NIST evidence concerns manufacturers generally, not this NAICS or LMM firms specifically.
Large public-company digital offerings indicate sector direction but likely run ahead of smaller manufacturers.
rho.change_evidence: NIST reports that 46% of manufacturers use AI tools or chatbots and more than 80% expect AI use to increase within two years, while documenting the practical barriers that keep adoption below the technical frontier.
rho.rationale: Five-year implementation should reach a meaningful but incomplete share of exposed tasks. Quality economics, skilled-labor scarcity, predictive-maintenance value, and digital customer requirements support adoption, while integration with legacy machinery, fragmented data, cybersecurity, capital requirements, and validation of safety-critical output slow deployment.

s5.bridge: Source population/quantity: U.S. employer-business owners across industries; 22% report a five-year sale or transfer plan and 52.3% of employer businesses have owners age 55 or older. Transformation: used the five-year intention as the central anchor and widened for execution failure and possible above-average succession pressure in specialized manufacturing. Error direction: intentions likely overstate completed qualifying transfers, while unplanned and health-triggered sales offset some downward conversion.
caveats: Owner intentions are not completed transactions.
The survey is not specific to manufacturing, this NAICS, or the EBITDA band.
s5.change_evidence: Gallup reports that 52.3% of U.S. employer businesses are owned by people age 55 or older and 22% of employer-business owners plan to sell or transfer within five years.
s5.rationale: Aging private ownership creates a material five-year control-transfer pool, but qualifying transfers exclude closures, minority recapitalizations, intra-family continuity without control change, and transactions outside the target size or lens.

principal_driver: A large physical installed base with recurring OEM parts and support can combine operational automation with defensible customer relationships.
principal_weakness: Capital-equipment demand is volatile and can be deferred sharply when oilfield customers cut drilling and completion budgets.

run_meta.title: Oil and Gas Field Machinery and Equipment Manufacturing

sources.supports: Broad machinery-manufacturing occupational mix used as the task-exposure proxy.

sources.title: The Rise of Artificial Intelligence (AI) in U.S. Manufacturing

sources.supports: Oilfield-equipment business model, aftermarket and digital services, OEM installed-base support, backlog, contract risks, customer demand drivers, rig counts, prices, and segment revenue mix.
sources.title: NOV Inc. 2025 Annual Report

sources.supports: U.S. crude-oil and natural-gas production outlook used to anchor the five-year real-demand proxy.
sources.title: EIA Releases Annual Energy Outlook 2026

title: Oil and Gas Field Machinery and Equipment Manufacturing
